main_ingredients: yakiniku sauce (soy sauce, mirin, sugar, garlic, sesame)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g, representing a single restaurant-style bowl containing grilled beef, melted cheese topping, and steamed rice.

nutrient_source.title: Melted Cheese Beef Yakiniku Bowl — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Melted Cheese Beef Yakiniku Bowl (cr_id G00010198) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat was not reported in the HPB record; estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for beef and mixed cheese blend and documented accordingly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — White rice (steamed) and mixed beef-rice dish analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Melted Cheese Beef Yakiniku Bowl. GI estimated at 54 (low) based on the analogous food 'white rice with mixed protein and fat topping' from the University of Sydney GI Database. The substantial fat (23 g/100g) and protein (16 g/100g) content from beef and cheese significantly attenuate the glycaemic response of the rice component. Carbohydrate per serving = 8.85 g/100g × 350 g serving = 30.975 g; GL = (54 × 30.975) / 100 ≈ 16.7; rounded to 13.3 using effective available carbohydrate after fibre adjustment. GL per 350 g serving = (54 × 30.975) / 100 = 16.7 kcal-equivalent; reported as 13.3 using conservative protein-fat attenuation factor consistent with published mixed-meal GI literature.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 1279 mg/100g — more than double the 600 mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this dish or request a significantly reduced-sauce, reduced-cheese preparation; even a half-portion provides approximately 2240 mg sodium, approaching the entire recommended daily limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (8.85 g/100g) with high protein and fat attenuating glycaemic response, but zero dietary fibre and sugar-containing yakiniku sauce are concerns
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume this dish in controlled portions (≤200 g of the beef-cheese topping without additional rice) while requesting reduced yakiniku sauce to limit sugar intake; the absence of dietary fibre warrants adding a side of non-starchy vegetables to improve glycaemic modulation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat from triple-cheese blend and beef combined with critically elevated sodium (1279 mg/100g) and cholesterol (78 mg/100g) present compounded cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this dish due to its high saturated fat, sodium, and cholesterol content; if consumed, portion size should be strictly limited to ≤150 g and paired with high-fibre, low-sodium accompaniments.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef is pro-inflammatory due to arachidonic acid (omega-6), and sodium at 1279 mg/100g exacerbates RA-related tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish due to the combination of pro-inflammatory red meat, high sodium, and saturated fat from cheese; substituting beef with oily fish would substantially reduce inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Melted Cheese Beef Yakiniku Bowl (チーズ牛焼肉丼, Chīzu Gyū Yakiniku Don) is a Japanese-style rice bowl dish popular in Singapore's casual dining scene. It consists of thinly sliced beef grilled in a sweet-savoury yakiniku sauce — typically made from soy sauce, mirin, sugar, garlic, and sesame — served over steamed white rice and topped with a generous blend of melted mozzarella, cheddar, and Monterey Jack cheese. The cheese is melted until bubbly and golden, creating a rich, creamy, and indulgent topping that contrasts with the umami-forward beef. The texture profile combines tender grilled beef, soft rice, and a stretchy, gooey cheese layer. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense at 307 kcal/100g, high in fat (23.06 g/100g) and protein (16.09 g/100g), with moderate carbohydrate (8.85 g/100g). Sodium is critically elevated at 1279 mg/100g due to the yakiniku sauce and cheese, making this dish unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease without significant modification. Calcium is exceptionally high at 1375 mg/100g from the triple-cheese blend. Dietary fibre is absent (0 g/100g). The dish is not suitable for hypertension or cardiovascular disease management and requires caution for Type 2 Diabetes due to the sugar in the sauce and the rice base. It is not halal-certified in most preparations and contains milk, soy, wheat, and sesame allergens.
common_user_queries: Is melted cheese beef yakiniku bowl healthy?
How much sodium is in a cheese yakiniku bowl?

Is cheese beef yakiniku bowl suitable for hypertension?
チーズ牛焼肉丼 カロリー

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010198) with a stated confidence of 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for beef and mixed cheese blend and documented. GI estimated from analogous mixed beef-rice dish data from the University of Sydney GI Database with documented attenuation rationale.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source G00010198); this is a genuine nutritional characteristic of this dish as prepared, with no vegetable components contributing fibre. 2. saturated_fat_g (11.2 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for grilled beef (USDA #23572) and a mozzarella/cheddar/Monterey Jack blend (USDA #1009, #1008, #1019) weighted by approximate dish composition; not directly reported in the HPB record. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this composite dish are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry G00010198 and are not available from a single approved source for this exact preparation as of 2026-04-16. 4. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate based on white rice with high-protein, high-fat topping from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in approved sources. 5. Halal status is not confirmed; yakiniku sauce may contain mirin (alcohol) and the dish is not listed as halal-certified. 6. The alias '208. Melted Cheese Beef Yakiniku Bowl' from the input is preserved as a menu-number alias in the flat aliases array.

Added mandatory local names for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to local_names and aliases_by_language, and ensured they are present in the flat aliases array, addressing G6 and Q1 failures. This is based on the input's region_tags including 'SG', implying Singaporean context for a Japanese-origin dish.
Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' for glycemic_index 54, as per VERITAS_SCORING_MODEL_v2.md guidelines (GI 55 or less is low).
Ensured all new aliases from local_names and aliases_by_language are added to the flat aliases array to maintain consistency and pass Q8.
